infobox.value: (**SEC**) is an [independent agency of the United States federal government](/wiki/Independent_agencies_of_the_United_States_government "Independent agencies of the United States government"), created in the aftermath of the [Wall Street crash of 1929](/wiki/Wall_Street_crash_of_1929 "Wall Street crash of 1929").[[9]](#cite_note-Britannica-SEC-9) Its primary purpose is to enforce the federal securities laws, regulate key parts of the [capital markets](/wiki/Capital_market "Capital market") industry, protect [investors](/wiki/Investor "Investor"), maintain fair, orderly, and [efficient markets](/wiki/Efficient-market_hypothesis "Efficient-market hypothesis"), and facilitate [capital formation](/wiki/Capital_formation "Capital formation").[[10]](#cite_note-Britannica-Regulators-10)[[9]](#cite_note-Britannica-SEC-9)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&redirect=no "U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission"))

U.S. federal agency policing and regulating capital markets

"Securities and Exchange Commission" redirects here. For other uses, see [Securities and Exchange Commission (disambiguation)](/wiki/Securities_and_Exchange_Commission_(disambiguation) "Securities and Exchange Commission (disambiguation)").

The **United States Securities and Exchange Commission** (**SEC**) is an [independent agency of the United States federal government](/wiki/Independent_agencies_of_the_United_States_government "Independent agencies of the United States government"), created in the aftermath of the [Wall Street crash of 1929](/wiki/Wall_Street_crash_of_1929 "Wall Street crash of 1929").[[9]](#cite_note-Britannica-SEC-9) Its primary purpose is to enforce the federal securities laws, regulate key parts of the [capital markets](/wiki/Capital_market "Capital market") industry, protect [investors](/wiki/Investor "Investor"), maintain fair, orderly, and [efficient markets](/wiki/Efficient-market_hypothesis "Efficient-market hypothesis"), and facilitate [capital formation](/wiki/Capital_formation "Capital formation").[[10]](#cite_note-Britannica-Regulators-10)[[9]](#cite_note-Britannica-SEC-9)

[Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") created the SEC in 1934 after the stock market crash of 1929 as part of [New Deal securities reforms](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal"). Section 4 of the [Securities Exchange Act of 1934](/wiki/Securities_Exchange_Act_of_1934 "Securities Exchange Act of 1934") established the agency. The SEC administers the principal federal securities laws, oversees disclosures by [public companies](/wiki/Public_company "Public company"), key market intermediaries, [investment products](/wiki/Investment_fund "Investment fund") and [trading venues](/wiki/Exchange_(organized_market) "Exchange (organized market)").[[11]](#cite_note-Britannica-ExchangeAct-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Seligman-TWS2-12) It also investigates and enforces against [misconduct](/wiki/Misconduct "Misconduct") such as [financial fraud](/wiki/Fraud "Fraud"), [insider trading](/wiki/Insider_trading "Insider trading"), and [market manipulation](/wiki/Market_manipulation "Market manipulation").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The SEC carries out its work through [rulemaking](/wiki/Rulemaking "Rulemaking"), examinations, and [enforcement actions](/wiki/Enforcement "Enforcement"). The agency enforces the securities laws primarily through civil actions in [federal court](/wiki/Federal_judiciary_of_the_United_States "Federal judiciary of the United States") or [administrative proceedings](/wiki/Administrative_court "Administrative court") and refers potential criminal violations to the [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation") or the [Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") when appropriate.[[14]](#cite_note-HazenSecurities-14) The agency has its [headquarters](/wiki/Headquarters "Headquarters") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), and is commonly known as 'the commission' or 'the agency'.

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

The SEC carries out its mandate through three recurring functions that span the regulatory lifecycle: *disclosure-based regulation* (to require standardized public reporting intended to reduce misinformation and help investors evaluate issuers and compare risks), *oversight of market structure* (to detect irregularities, supervise key market participants and trading venues), and *civil enforcement* (to investigate potential violations, bring enforcement actions that sanction misconduct, and deter future violations).[[15]](#cite_note-Goshen-Parchomovsky-15)[[16]](#cite_note-GAO-FINRA-2024-16)[[14]](#cite_note-HazenSecurities-14) Disclosure rules require standardized reporting intended to help investors compare issuers and evaluate risk. Market oversight focuses on supervised entities such as broker-dealers, investment advisers, and exchanges. Enforcement supports market integrity by investigating potential violations and bringing civil actions involving misconduct such as fraud or [insider trading](/wiki/Insider_trading "Insider trading"). The SEC’s mission can involve trade-offs.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Disclosure includes the statutory requirement that [public companies](/wiki/Public_company "Public company") and other regulated entities submit quarterly, annual, and other periodic disclosures.[[14]](#cite_note-HazenSecurities-14)[[18]](#cite_note-Deloitte-MDA-18) In addition to annual [financial reports](/wiki/Financial_statements "Financial statements"), company executives must provide a narrative account, called the "[management discussion and analysis](/wiki/Management_discussion_and_analysis "Management discussion and analysis")" (MD&A), that reviews the previous year of operations and explains the company's financial condition and results. MD&A may also discuss known trends, risks, and near-term plans. Since 1994, most registration statements and related materials filed with the SEC have been available through the SEC's online system, [EDGAR](/wiki/EDGAR "EDGAR") (the Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval system).[[19]](#cite_note-Spamann-Wilkinson-EDGAR-19)

Market oversight focuses on the SEC's supervision of key market participants and trading venues, including [broker-dealers](/wiki/Broker-dealer "Broker-dealer"), [investment advisers](/wiki/Investment_adviser "Investment adviser"), exchanges, clearing agencies, and other regulated entities. Through examinations, monitoring, and oversight of market structure, the SEC works to detect irregularities, promote compliance, and help maintain fair and orderly markets.[[10]](#cite_note-Britannica-Regulators-10)[[16]](#cite_note-GAO-FINRA-2024-16) Unlike bank deposits, investments in [capital markets](/wiki/Capital_market "Capital market") are not [guaranteed](/wiki/Federal_Deposit_Insurance_Corporation "Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation") by the federal government, so oversight and transparency are intended to help investors make more informed decisions and to reduce risks such as [insider trading](/wiki/Insider_trading "Insider trading"), [fraud](/wiki/Fraud "Fraud"), and market abuse.

The SEC’s enforcement efforts aim to identify and stop violations of the securities laws, hold wrongdoers accountable, and deter future misconduct.[[14]](#cite_note-HazenSecurities-14)[[20]](#cite_note-GAO-SEC-2025-Audit22-20) Enforcement staff may use information available through [EDGAR](/wiki/Electronic_Data_Gathering,_Analysis,_and_Retrieval "Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval"), while tips, complaints, and referrals are submitted through the SEC’s online TCR system.[[19]](#cite_note-Spamann-Wilkinson-EDGAR-19)[[21]](#cite_note-21) The SEC generally does not comment on whether it has opened an investigation in a particular matter or on the status of its investigations.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### State regulation and the limits of blue sky laws

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: State regulation and the limits of blue sky laws")]

Before the enactment of the federal securities laws and the creation of the SEC, securities trading was governed by so-called [blue sky laws](/wiki/Blue_sky_law "Blue sky law"), a nickname that referred to speculative schemes sold as having nothing behind them but "blue sky."[[23]](#cite_note-bluesky2-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Mahoney-BlueSky-24)[[25]](#cite_note-Warren-DualReg-25) States enacted and enforced these laws, which regulated the offering and sale of securities to protect the public from fraud.

Although the specific provisions varied among states, these laws generally required the registration of all securities offerings and sales, as well as every U.S. [stockbroker](/wiki/Stockbroker "Stockbroker") and brokerage firm.[[26]](#cite_note-bluesky-26) However, blue sky laws were generally considered ineffective. For example, as early as 1915, the Investment Bankers Association told its members that they could circumvent blue sky laws by making securities offerings across state lines through the mail.[[27]](#cite_note-transwall-27) By the early twentieth century, critics argued that the states lacked a strong and uniform framework for policing a growing national securities market.[[27]](#cite_note-transwall-27)[[28]](#cite_note-Seligman-TWS2-3952-28)[[29]](#cite_note-DiTrolio-BlueSky-29)

### Wall Street 1929 crash and the federal turn

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Wall Street 1929 crash and the federal turn")]

See also: [Securities Act of 1933](/wiki/Securities_Act_of_1933 "Securities Act of 1933") and [Securities Exchange Act of 1934](/wiki/Securities_Exchange_Act_of_1934 "Securities Exchange Act of 1934")

The modern federal securities system emerged out of the economic and political upheaval that followed the [Wall Street crash of 1929](/wiki/Wall_Street_crash_of_1929 "Wall Street crash of 1929").[[30]](#cite_note-Perino-Hellhound-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Moss-Pecora-31)[[9]](#cite_note-Britannica-SEC-9)[[11]](#cite_note-Britannica-ExchangeAct-11) After the [Pecora Commission](/wiki/Pecora_Commission "Pecora Commission") hearings on abuses and frauds in securities markets, Congress passed the [Securities Act of 1933](/wiki/Securities_Act_of_1933 "Securities Act of 1933") ([15 U.S.C.](/wiki/Title_15_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 15 of the United States Code") [§ 77a](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/15/77a)), which federally regulates [original issues](/wiki/Primary_market "Primary market") of securities across state lines, primarily by requiring that issuing companies register distributions prior to sale so that investors may access basic financial information and make informed decisions.[[32]](#cite_note-Mahoney-1933-32)[[12]](#cite_note-Seligman-TWS2-12) For the first year, the [Federal Trade Commission](/wiki/Federal_Trade_Commission "Federal Trade Commission") administered the statute.[[11]](#cite_note-Britannica-ExchangeAct-11)[[28]](#cite_note-Seligman-TWS2-3952-28)

The following year, Congress passed the [Securities Exchange Act of 1934](/wiki/Securities_Exchange_Act_of_1934 "Securities Exchange Act of 1934") to regulate the so-called "secondary market." The “secondary market” refers to trading among investors after securities are first issued, where buyers and sellers transact with each other (not the issuer) on exchanges and other venues. Section 4 of the 1934 Act also formally established the SEC[[9]](#cite_note-Britannica-SEC-9)[[11]](#cite_note-Britannica-ExchangeAct-11)[[9]](#cite_note-Britannica-SEC-9)[[11]](#cite_note-Britannica-ExchangeAct-11) and brought exchanges, broker-dealers, and other key market participants under federal oversight.[[11]](#cite_note-Britannica-ExchangeAct-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Seligman-TWS2-12)[[11]](#cite_note-Britannica-ExchangeAct-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Seligman-TWS2-12)

### Early Commission and New Deal expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Early Commission and New Deal expansion")]

[![Portrait of Joseph P. Kennedy Sr.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Joseph_P._Kennedy%2C_Sr._1940.jpg/250px-Joseph_P._Kennedy%2C_Sr._1940.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joseph_P._Kennedy,_Sr._1940.jpg)

Joseph P. Kennedy Sr., first SEC chair (photo circa 1940).

[![Time magazine cover featuring Joseph P. Kennedy Sr. (1935).](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Joseph_P._Kennedy-TIME-1935.jpg/250px-Joseph_P._Kennedy-TIME-1935.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joseph_P._Kennedy-TIME-1935.jpg)

Kennedy on the cover of *Time* (1935).

In 1934, Roosevelt named his friend [Joseph P. Kennedy](/wiki/Joseph_P._Kennedy "Joseph P. Kennedy") as the first chair of the SEC. Kennedy was a wealthy financier a self-made millionaire, and leader among the Irish-American community. The president chose Kennedy partly based on his experience on Wall Street.[[33]](#cite_note-Nasaw-2262-33) Roosevelt believed that experience would help give the new agency credibility on Wall Street as well as with the public.[[34]](#cite_note-Nasaw-Kennedy-34)

The early Commission also drew in a number of younger lawyers and officials who later became nationally prominent, including [William O. Douglas](/wiki/William_O._Douglas "William O. Douglas"), [Abe Fortas](/wiki/Abe_Fortas "Abe Fortas"), [James M. Landis](/wiki/James_M._Landis "James M. Landis"), and [Ferdinand Pecora](/wiki/Ferdinand_Pecora "Ferdinand Pecora").[[34]](#cite_note-Nasaw-Kennedy-34)[[35]](#cite_note-McCraw-1982-35) Historical accounts describe the early SEC as one of the more successful New Deal regulatory agencies, combining disclosure-based regulation, market oversight, and enforcement in a way that helped legitimize permanent federal supervision of the securities markets.[[35]](#cite_note-McCraw-1982-35)

Over time, other statutes such as the [Trust Indenture Act of 1939](/wiki/Trust_Indenture_Act_of_1939 "Trust Indenture Act of 1939"), the [Investment Company Act of 1940](/wiki/Investment_Company_Act_of_1940 "Investment Company Act of 1940"), and the [Investment Advisers Act of 1940](/wiki/Investment_Advisers_Act_of_1940 "Investment Advisers Act of 1940"), the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 expanded the SEC's authority into areas including public public reporting, debt offerings, mutual funds, investment companies, investment advisers.[[12]](#cite_note-Seligman-TWS2-12)

### Postwar capital markets growth and mid-century reform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Postwar capital markets growth and mid-century reform")]

After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the SEC increasingly faced a [securities market](/wiki/Securities_market "Securities market") that was larger, more national, and more institutionally complex than the one it had inherited in the 1930s.[[36]](#cite_note-Langevoort-2009-36)[[37]](#cite_note-Gadsby-1970-37)[[36]](#cite_note-Langevoort-2009-36) Postwar [trading volume](/wiki/Volume_(finance) "Volume (finance)") rose sharply, the [over-the-counter market](/wiki/Over-the-counter_(finance) "Over-the-counter (finance)") grew, and the agency's workload expanded with the market.[[37]](#cite_note-Gadsby-1970-37) A major turning point came in the early 1960s, when Congress directed the SEC to conduct a broad investigation of [market structure](/wiki/Market_structure "Market structure") and [investor protection](/wiki/Investor "Investor") that became known as the *Special Study of Securities Markets*.[[38]](#cite_note-Fox-2018-38)[[37]](#cite_note-Gadsby-1970-37)[[38]](#cite_note-Fox-2018-38) Later accounts describe the study as a response to rapid market growth, changing trading practices, and a sense that the SEC lacked the current data needed for major reform.[[37]](#cite_note-Gadsby-1970-37) Scholarship has described the study as a seminal moment in the history of U.S. [securities regulation](/wiki/Financial_regulation "Financial regulation") and a key inflection point between the [New Deal framework](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") and the SEC's later market-structure reforms.[[38]](#cite_note-Fox-2018-38)[[39]](#cite_note-Pritchard-Thompson-2018-39) The study documented weaknesses in areas including the over-the-counter market, [broker-dealer supervision](/wiki/Broker-dealer "Broker-dealer"), and exchange self-regulation, and it supplied much of the intellectual basis for the Securities Acts Amendments of 1964.[[37]](#cite_note-Gadsby-1970-37)[[39]](#cite_note-Pritchard-Thompson-2018-39) Those 1964 amendments extended periodic reporting, proxy disclosure, and insider-ownership reporting requirements to many larger over-the-counter issuers, thereby broadening the federal disclosure system beyond exchange-listed companies.[[40]](#cite_note-Greenstone-1964-40)[[39]](#cite_note-Pritchard-Thompson-2018-39)

### Paperwork crisis and investor protection reform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Paperwork crisis and investor protection reform")]

By the late 1960s, trading volume rose sharply, and the industry faced what contemporaries and later accounts called the “paperwork crisis,” also referred to as the “paper crunch” or the “back-office crisis.”[[41]](#cite_note-Ledrut-Upper-2007-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Donald-2007-42) The terms describe the inability of broker-dealers’ back offices to process and settle a rapidly increasing number of trades at a time when settlement still relied on paper certificates and manual procedure.[[41]](#cite_note-Ledrut-Upper-2007-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Donald-2007-42)

Trading volume was central to the problem. Average daily volume on [the New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") increased from just over 3 million shares in 1960 to more than 13 million in 1968, overwhelming many firms' operational capacity.[[43]](#cite_note-Fons-1987-43)[[41]](#cite_note-Ledrut-Upper-2007-41) The strain led to widespread settlement delays, record-keeping errors, and financial distress, followed by a wave of mergers, liquidations, and failures among securities firms.[[41]](#cite_note-Ledrut-Upper-2007-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Donald-2007-42)

Congress responded with the [Securities Investor Protection Act](/wiki/Securities_Investor_Protection_Act "Securities Investor Protection Act") of 1970, which created the [Securities Investor Protection Corporation](/wiki/Securities_Investor_Protection_Corporation "Securities Investor Protection Corporation") (SIPC). SIPC—a nonprofit membership corporation for most registered broker-dealers—was intended to return missing cash and securities to customers of failed firms more quickly than ordinary bankruptcy proceedings and, in doing so, help restore investor confidence after the breakdowns of 1968–1970.[[43]](#cite_note-Fons-1987-43)

### 1970s market-structure overhaul

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1970s market-structure overhaul")]

The paperwork crisis did not, by itself, lead to the next major reforms, but it was one of the problems that pushed [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") and the SEC to rethink how the [securities markets](/wiki/Capital_market "Capital market") worked.[[42]](#cite_note-Donald-2007-42)[[44]](#cite_note-Peirce-NMS-2018-44)[[45]](#cite_note-Mahoney-Rauterberg-2018-45)

The [Securities Acts Amendments of 1975](/wiki/Securities_Acts_Amendments_of_1975 "Securities Acts Amendments of 1975") told the SEC to help create a [National Market System](/wiki/National_Market_System "National Market System") and set the stage for major changes in trading, competition, and clearance and settlement.[[44]](#cite_note-Peirce-NMS-2018-44)[[45]](#cite_note-Mahoney-Rauterberg-2018-45) Some people viewed the 1975 amendments as both deregulatory[[46]](#cite_note-Macey-Haddock-1985-46) and the biggest restructuring of the securities industry since 1934.[[47]](#cite_note-Gillis-Dreher-1982-47)[[44]](#cite_note-Peirce-NMS-2018-44)

One of the clearest results was the end of fixed brokerage commission rates. Several forces drove that change: institutional investors made up a growing share of securities trading,[[44]](#cite_note-Peirce-NMS-2018-44)[[48]](#cite_note-Traflet-Coyne-2007-48) third-market trading and regional exchanges added competitive pressure,[[45]](#cite_note-Mahoney-Rauterberg-2018-45)[[49]](#cite_note-Jarrell-1984-49)[[44]](#cite_note-Peirce-NMS-2018-44) and regulators and antitrust officials pushed back against a pricing system that many saw as unfair and inefficient.[[48]](#cite_note-Traflet-Coyne-2007-48)[[50]](#cite_note-Baxter-1970-50)[[49]](#cite_note-Jarrell-1984-49)[[48]](#cite_note-Traflet-Coyne-2007-48) As one sign of that shift, institutional investors' share of NYSE trading volume rose from 34 percent in 1961 to 70 percent in 1974.[[44]](#cite_note-Peirce-NMS-2018-44)

### 1980s takeover and insider-trading era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1980s takeover and insider-trading era")]

In the 1980s, takeover fights and [insider trading](/wiki/Insider_trading "Insider trading") scandals became major issues for [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") and the SEC.[[51]](#cite_note-Bhagat-1990-51)[[52]](#cite_note-Joo-2007-52) Studies of the period describe [hostile takeovers](/wiki/Takeover "Takeover"), [leveraged buyouts](/wiki/Leveraged_buyout "Leveraged buyout"), and [junk-bond finance](/wiki/High-yield_debt "High-yield debt") as defining features of the decade's market for corporate control.[[51]](#cite_note-Bhagat-1990-51) A later Brookings study looked at 62 hostile takeover contests between 1984 and 1986 involving targets with purchase prices of $50 million or more, showing how common takeover battles had become in that period.[[51]](#cite_note-Bhagat-1990-51)

Meanwhile, Congress zeroed in on [insider trading](/wiki/Insider_trading "Insider trading"). It passed new insider trading laws in 1984 and 1988 and held four sets of hearings on the topic between 1986 and 1988.[[52]](#cite_note-Joo-2007-52)[[53]](#cite_note-Becker-2012-53)

Those laws raised civil and [criminal penalties](/wiki/Penalties "Penalties") for insider trading.[[52]](#cite_note-Joo-2007-52)[[53]](#cite_note-Becker-2012-53) As a result, the SEC started seeing a lot more insider-trading cases tied to hostile takeovers in its enforcement work.[[52]](#cite_note-Joo-2007-52)[[53]](#cite_note-Becker-2012-53)

### Disclosure modernization and EDGAR

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Disclosure modernization and EDGAR")]

By the late 1980s and early 1990s, the SEC was also changing how it handled corporate disclosure. For many years, the agency had relied mainly on a paper filing system, which made review, storage, and public access slower and harder to manage. Before [EDGAR](/wiki/Electronic_Data_Gathering,_Analysis,_and_Retrieval "Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval"), investors often had to visit SEC public reference rooms in person or pay commercial data vendors to get filings, which made access more limited and uneven.[[54]](#cite_note-Goldstein-EDGAR-54)

As securities markets grew and the number of filings increased, the SEC moved toward electronic filing and distribution as part of a broader effort to modernize its disclosure system.[[55]](#cite_note-vanDuren-EDGAR-55)

That shift led to the development and rollout of [EDGAR](/wiki/EDGAR "EDGAR") (Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval). The SEC began testing an [electronic filing](/wiki/Electronic_Filing_System "Electronic Filing System") pilot in 1984, moved to phased mandatory filing in 1993, and completed the transition for U.S. reporting companies in 1996.[[55]](#cite_note-vanDuren-EDGAR-55)[[54]](#cite_note-Goldstein-EDGAR-54) EDGAR made registration statements, annual reports, quarterly reports, and other corporate filings available to the public much faster and more widely than the older paper system had allowed.[[55]](#cite_note-vanDuren-EDGAR-55)[[54]](#cite_note-Goldstein-EDGAR-54)

The change did not alter the SEC's basic disclosure-based approach to securities regulation, but it did change how filings were submitted, reviewed, released, and accessed by investors, analysts, journalists, and the public. Later accounts have described EDGAR as one of the SEC's most important administrative and information-system changes of the late twentieth century because it sharply reduced access costs and made corporate disclosure much easier to search and use on a national scale.[[54]](#cite_note-Goldstein-EDGAR-54)[[55]](#cite_note-vanDuren-EDGAR-55)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In the 21st century, Congress expanded the SEC’s authority in response to major [corporate scandals](/wiki/List_of_corporate_collapses_and_scandals "List of corporate collapses and scandals") and [financial crises](/wiki/Financial_crisis "Financial crisis"). After the collapses of [Enron](/wiki/Enron_scandal "Enron scandal") and [WorldCom](/wiki/WorldCom_scandal "WorldCom scandal"), [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") enacted the [Sarbanes–Oxley Act of 2002](/wiki/Sarbanes%E2%80%93Oxley_Act "Sarbanes–Oxley Act").[[56]](#cite_note-Britannica-SOX-56) The legislative act created the [Public Company Accounting Oversight Board](/wiki/Public_Company_Accounting_Oversight_Board "Public Company Accounting Oversight Board") and strengthened requirements for [auditor oversight](/wiki/International_Forum_of_Independent_Audit_Regulators "International Forum of Independent Audit Regulators"), [internal controls](/wiki/Internal_control "Internal control"), and [auditor independence](/wiki/Auditor_independence "Auditor independence").

[![Physical tokens with Bitcoin and Ethereum symbols.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/President_Obama_Signs_the_Dodd-Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act_%284816864266%29.jpg/250px-President_Obama_Signs_the_Dodd-Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act_%284816864266%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Obama_Signs_the_Dodd-Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act_(4816864266).jpg)

[Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") signs the Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (2010).

Following the [financial crisis of 2007–2008](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), Congress passed the [Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act of 2010](/wiki/Dodd%E2%80%93Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act "Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act"), which broadened the SEC’s authority in areas including [private-fund](/wiki/Private_Fund_Investment_Advisers_Registration_Act "Private Fund Investment Advisers Registration Act") advisers, [credit rating agencies](/wiki/Credit_rating_agency "Credit rating agency"), and parts of the derivatives and clearing framework.[[57]](#cite_note-Britannica-DoddFrank-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) The law also created the Office of Financial Research within the [Treasury Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") to support the [Financial Stability Oversight Council](/wiki/Financial_Stability_Oversight_Council "Financial Stability Oversight Council").[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

In the 2020s, the SEC placed greater emphasis on equity-market structure and [digital assets](/wiki/Cryptocurrency "Cryptocurrency"). The [January 2021 GameStop “meme stock” episode](/wiki/GameStop_short_squeeze "GameStop short squeeze") revealed how digital platforms, mobile apps, and social media can quickly alter trading dynamics and liquidity.[[61]](#cite_note-Fisch20222-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62) The episode drew attention to issues such as brokerage trading restrictions, payment for [order flow](/wiki/Order_flow_trading "Order flow trading"), [dark pools](/wiki/Dark_pool "Dark pool") and wholesalers, and [short-selling practices](/wiki/Short_(finance) "Short (finance)").[[63]](#cite_note-63) In the years that followed, the SEC adopted or proposed changes addressing [order-execution disclosures](/wiki/In-order_execution "In-order execution"), [best execution obligations](/wiki/Best_execution "Best execution"), and retail order competition as part of a broader market structure effort.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

In parallel, cross-border oversight became more central as foreign issuers relied on U.S. markets while audit access disputes persisted. That emphasis sharpened in the early 2020s as the SEC implemented the [Holding Foreign Companies Accountable Act](/wiki/Holding_Foreign_Companies_Accountable_Act "Holding Foreign Companies Accountable Act") framework. The HFCAA approach linked U.S. market access more directly to audit-inspection requirements, including the ability to inspect relevant audit work papers for certain foreign issuers.["SEC Implements Holding Foreign Companies Accountable Act"](https://www.wsj.com/articles/sec-implements-holding-foreign-companies-accountable-act-116). *The Wall Street Journal*. Dow Jones & Company. Retrieved February 5, 2026."

As the market for [digital assets](/wiki/Cryptocurrency "Cryptocurrency") grew, the SEC increased its focus on cryptocurrency and [blockchain-based issues](/wiki/Blockchain "Blockchain"). The agency took enforcement actions tied to the [FTX collapse](/wiki/Bankruptcy_of_FTX "Bankruptcy of FTX") and the Terraform Labs. These case fed ongoing debates about how to regulate new technologies like cryptocurrency, [decentralized finance (DeFi)](/wiki/Decentralized_finance "Decentralized finance"), and [stablecoins](/wiki/Stablecoin "Stablecoin"). They also raised practical questions about how disclosure, [custody](/wiki/Custodian_bank "Custodian bank"), [conflict-of-interest issues](/wiki/Conflict_of_interest "Conflict of interest") translate to [token-based products](/wiki/Tokenized_real-world_asset "Tokenized real-world asset") and services.

#### Enforcement tensions under Paul Atkins (2025–2026)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Enforcement tensions under Paul Atkins (2025–2026)")]

In March 2026, Reuters reported that Margaret Ryan, the head of the SEC's enforcement unit, resigned after clashes with senior agency leadership over the direction of the enforcement program, including the handling of key investigations involving [Justin Sun](/wiki/Justin_Sun "Justin Sun") and [Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk").[[65]](#cite_note-Reuters-Ryan-2026-65) One widely reported episode involved the SEC's 2025 lawsuit over Musk's 2022 purchases of [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") shares. The SEC filed the federal action in the [District Court for the District of Columbia](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_District_of_Columbia "United States District Court for the District of Columbia") in 2025, alleging that Musk had failed to disclose promptly that he had crossed the 5 percent ownership threshold, which allowed him to buy additional shares at artificially low prices.[[66]](#cite_note-Reuters-Musk-Dismissal-2026-66) In February 2026, Judge [Sparkle Sooknanan](/wiki/Sparkle_Sooknanan "Sparkle Sooknanan") denied Musk's motion to dismiss. Reuters reported that the court rejected Musk's selective-enforcement arguments,[[66]](#cite_note-Reuters-Musk-Dismissal-2026-66) and at a later hearing Sooknanan described those arguments as "a stretch".[[67]](#cite_note-SECvMusk-Transcript-2026-67) The same hearing drew notice because of a dispute over settlement talks. Sarah Concannon, Musk's counsel, told the court that the parties had been attempting to resolve the case even though the SEC's counsel of record had not been "fully read in on that"; the *Financial Times* later reported that Margaret Ryan was among the SEC officials who had not been read in on those discussions. Sooknanan called that "odd", asked follow-up questions about who had been involved in the discussions, and declined Concannon's request for a sidebar.[[67]](#cite_note-SECvMusk-Transcript-2026-67)[[68]](#cite_note-FT-Musk-SEC-2026-68) On March 16, 2026, the SEC announced that Ryan had resigned from her position, effective immediately.[[69]](#cite_note-SEC-Ryan-2026-69)

## Organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Organization")]

The agency is supported by headquarters divisions, specialized offices, and a network of regional offices.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71) The SEC’s [headquarters](/wiki/Headquarters "Headquarters") includes six principal divisions—Corporation Finance, Trading and Markets, Investment Management, Enforcement, Economic and Risk Analysis, and Examinations—supported by specialized offices.

The agency also operates a network of regional offices that extends [examinations](/wiki/Regulatory_compliance "Regulatory compliance") and [enforcement activities](/wiki/Federal_law_enforcement_in_the_United_States "Federal law enforcement in the United States") beyond [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")

### Commission composition and members

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Commission composition and members")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of members of the Securities and Exchange Commission](/wiki/List_of_members_of_the_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "List of members of the Securities and Exchange Commission").

A five-member commission, appointed by the president of the United States with the advice and consent of the Senate, governs the SEC. Its members vote on major agency actions. No more than three commissioners may belong to the same political party. Commissioners serve five-year terms, and the terms are staggered so that one expires each year. A commissioner may continue to serve until a successor is appointed and has qualified, but not beyond the expiration of the next session of Congress following the end of the fixed term.[[72]](#cite_note-72) The [Securities Exchange Act of 1934](/wiki/Securities_Exchange_Act_of_1934 "Securities Exchange Act of 1934") does not expressly provide a presidential removal standard for SEC commissioners, but SEC commissioners have traditionally been understood to be removable only for cause.[[73]](#cite_note-Payne-Removal-73)[[74]](#cite_note-ConLaw-Removal-74)

### The chair position

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: The chair position")]

The [president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") designates one of the commissioners to serve as the agency's chair, the SEC's top executive.[[77]](#cite_note-77) Under the Commission's rules, the chair exercises the Commission's executive and administrative functions, and the SEC's organizational structure places the agency's divisions and offices under the Office of the Chair.[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79) Major Commission decisions, orders, and rules are taken by the Commission itself and recorded in published Commission votes.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of Chairs of the Securities and Exchange Commission](/wiki/List_of_Chairs_of_the_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "List of Chairs of the Securities and Exchange Commission").

### Headquarters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Headquarters")]

Since 2005, the SEC has been headquartered at 100 F Street NE in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), just north of [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(Washington,_D.C.) "Union Station (Washington, D.C.)").[[81]](#cite_note-RollCall-2005-StationPlace-81)[[82]](#cite_note-SEC-HQ-Directions-82) The headquarters building includes an atrium lobby with a full-height curved glass wall at the main entrance.[[83]](#cite_note-WaPo-2005-Forgey-83)[[84]](#cite_note-Enclos-StationPlace-84)

[![1935 view of the Department of Labor and Interstate Commerce Commission buildings in Washington, D.C.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/DOL_%26_ICC_Buildings_Washington_DC_1935.jpg/330px-DOL_%26_ICC_Buildings_Washington_DC_1935.jpg)](/wiki/File:DOL_%26_ICC_Buildings_Washington_DC_1935.jpg)

Aerial view of the Interstate Commerce Commission Building (ICC) (right), the SEC's initial headquarters. Washington, DC, 1935,

[![Close view of the Constitution Avenue elevation of the Interstate Commerce Commission Building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Close_view_of_the_Constitution_Avenue_elevation_to_show_arched_gateways_and_pedimented_pavilion_%28%22Commerce_and_Communication%22_sculpted_pediment%29_-_Interstate_Commerce_Commission%2C_HABS_DC-871-2.tif/lossy-page1-330px-thumbnail.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Close_view_of_the_Constitution_Avenue_elevation_to_show_arched_gateways_and_pedimented_pavilion_(%22Commerce_and_Communication%22_sculpted_pediment)_-_Interstate_Commerce_Commission,_HABS_DC-871-2.tif)

Front facade of the ICC building showing the arched gateways and pedimented pavilions.

The agency's headquarters has moved several times since its creation in 1934. Its original headquarters was at 1778 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, at the corner of [Pennsylvania Avenue](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Avenue_National_Historic_Site "Pennsylvania Avenue National Historic Site") and [18th Street](/wiki/18th_Street_NW "18th Street NW"), about a block west of [the White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"). The SEC occupied the former [Interstate Commerce Commission](/wiki/Interstate_Commerce_Commission "Interstate Commerce Commission") building there from 1934 to 1942.[[85]](#cite_note-Smeltzer-Memoir-85) Historical accounts from the agency's early years describe dark hallways, beaverboard walls, and greasy windows.[[86]](#cite_note-SECHS-Esprit-86) The building was a [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") office structure that formed part of the broader [Federal Triangle](/wiki/Federal_Triangle "Federal Triangle") project in Washington.[[87]](#cite_note-PCAD-ICC-87)

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the SEC moved from Washington to [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") in 1942 to make room for war-related agencies.[[85]](#cite_note-Smeltzer-Memoir-85)[[88]](#cite_note-Atkins-Bondi-2008-88) The agency had fewer staff during the war years and moved into the former *Pennsylvania Athletic Club* on [Rittenhouse Square](/wiki/Rittenhouse_Square "Rittenhouse Square"), a thirteen-story clubhouse building. Historical accounts described it as "more like an older luxury hotel with athletic facilities than purely an athletic club"; the swimming pool was boarded over and turned into a file room, while exercise rooms and meeting rooms became hearing rooms.[[85]](#cite_note-Smeltzer-Memoir-85)

When the SEC returned to Washington in 1948, it moved into a temporary wartime building at 2nd and E Streets NW. Staff nicknamed it the “*Tarpaper Shack*,” a common term at the time for a cheap, temporary building.[[85]](#cite_note-Smeltzer-Memoir-85) The nickname reflected how plain and temporary the building seemed, not necessarily how it was built. In 1966, the SEC moved again, this time to a new eight-story office building at 500 North Capitol Street NW, across from Union Station and the city post office; the agency stayed there for sixteen years.[[85]](#cite_note-Smeltzer-Memoir-85) In 1982, the SEC consolidated its headquarters operations at 450 Fifth Street NW in [Judiciary Plaza](/wiki/Judiciary_Square "Judiciary Square"), in the Judiciary Square area of Washington, and remained there until moving to Station Place, its present headquarters.[[85]](#cite_note-Smeltzer-Memoir-85)[[89]](#cite_note-WP-1994-SEC-HQ-89) A 2025 presentation to the [Commission of Fine Arts](/wiki/Commission_of_Fine_Arts "Commission of Fine Arts") described 450 Fifth Street NW as an 11-story [Brutalist-style](/wiki/Brutalist_architecture "Brutalist architecture") office building.[[90]](#cite_note-CFA-2025-450Fifth-90) The building later became known as the Liberty Square Building and later housed offices of the [United States Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice").[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92)

### Divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Divisions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Front_fa%C3%A7ade_of_the_U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission_Headquarters.jpg/500px-Front_fa%C3%A7ade_of_the_U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission_Headquarters.jpg)](/wiki/File:Front_fa%C3%A7ade_of_the_U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission_Headquarters.jpg)

U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission headquarters, [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")

Six principal divisions headquartered in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") carry out the SEC's core work: [[93]](#cite_note-Divisions-GAO26-FinAudit-93)[[94]](#cite_note-Divisions-GAO25-AFR-HQ-94)

Corporation Finance
:   Corporation Finance oversees disclosures by [public companies](/wiki/Public_company "Public company") and reviews transactions such as mergers and securities offerings. The division also plays a central role in the SEC’s filing-review process and public-company reporting system, including [EDGAR](/wiki/EDGAR "EDGAR").[[95]](#cite_note-Divisions-GAO24-Fintech-95)[[96]](#cite_note-Divisions-GAO18-Climate-96)

Trading and Markets
:   The Division of Trading and Markets regulates major securities-market participants, including [broker-dealers](/wiki/Broker-dealer "Broker-dealer"), self-regulatory organizations (such as [FINRA](/wiki/Financial_Industry_Regulatory_Authority "Financial Industry Regulatory Authority")), clearing agencies, and transfer agents. It reviews proposed rule changes filed by self-regulatory organizations and helps the Commission oversee market structure and trading practices.[[97]](#cite_note-Divisions-GAO25-FINRA-97)[[98]](#cite_note-Divisions-FJC-FedSec-98) Broker-dealers that conduct securities business with the investing public generally must register with FINRA. Individuals associated with member firms who engage in the securities business must qualify and register with FINRA, including by passing the required examinations.[[98]](#cite_note-Divisions-FJC-FedSec-98)

Investment Management
:   The Division of Investment Management oversees registered investment companies, including [mutual funds](/wiki/Mutual_fund "Mutual fund") and other funds, as well as registered [investment advisers](/wiki/Investment_adviser "Investment adviser"). It develops regulatory policy under the [Investment Company Act of 1940](/wiki/Investment_Company_Act_of_1940 "Investment Company Act of 1940") and the [Investment Advisers Act of 1940](/wiki/Investment_Advisers_Act_of_1940 "Investment Advisers Act of 1940") and advises the Commission on rules and forms affecting the asset-management industry.[[95]](#cite_note-Divisions-GAO24-Fintech-95)[[99]](#cite_note-Divisions-GAO25-IM-99)

Enforcement
:   The Enforcement Division investigates violations of the securities laws and regulations to bring legal actions against alleged violators. It is the largest division in terms of allocated budget.[[95]](#cite_note-Divisions-GAO24-Fintech-95)[[100]](#cite_note-Divisions-GAO25-AFR-Costs-100) The SEC can bring a [civil action](/wiki/Lawsuit "Lawsuit") in a [U.S. District Court](/wiki/United_States_district_court "United States district court"), or an [administrative proceeding](/wiki/Administrative_proceeding "Administrative proceeding") heard by an independent [administrative law judge](/wiki/Administrative_law_judge "Administrative law judge") (ALJ). It also works with criminal authorities when parallel or related criminal cases are appropriate.[[95]](#cite_note-Divisions-GAO24-Fintech-95)[[98]](#cite_note-Divisions-FJC-FedSec-98)

Economic and Risk Analysis
:   The Division of Economic and Risk Analysis (DERA) conducts [economic](/wiki/Economics "Economics"), [advanced statistics](/wiki/Statistics "Statistics"), and [econometric analysis](/wiki/Econometrics "Econometrics"). It also provides [data science](/wiki/Data_science "Data science") and [data analytics](/wiki/Analytics "Analytics") across the agency. DERA is staffed by [PhD-trained](/wiki/Doctor_of_Philosophy "Doctor of Philosophy") [economists](/wiki/Economist "Economist"), [statisticians](/wiki/Statistician "Statistician"), and [mathematicians](/wiki/Mathematician "Mathematician"). The division's staff conducts advanced empirical and quantitative analysis in support of SEC rulemaking, risk assessment, and law enforcement investigations, including market analysis, economic modeling, and litigation-related work to support rulemaking, examinations, and law enforcement.[[95]](#cite_note-Divisions-GAO24-Fintech-95)[[101]](#cite_note-Divisions-GAO25-AFR-DERA-101) DERA also houses the agency's chief economist.[[102]](#cite_note-Divisions-GAO25-AFR-ChiefEconomist-102)

Examinations
:   The Division of Examinations conducts the SEC’s nationwide examination program. It uses risk-focused strategies intended to improve compliance, prevent fraud, monitor risk, and inform policy.[[97]](#cite_note-Divisions-GAO25-FINRA-97)[[103]](#cite_note-Divisions-GAO25-AFR-EXAMS-103)

### Field offices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Field offices")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/US_SEC_New_York_Field_Office_foyer.jpg/250px-US_SEC_New_York_Field_Office_foyer.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_SEC_New_York_Field_Office_foyer.jpg)

[Foyer](/wiki/Lobby_(room) "Lobby (room)") of the SEC’s [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") Field Office in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan")’s [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_Manhattan "Financial District, Manhattan").

The SEC maintains 10 regional offices across the United States, which extend the agency’s enforcement and examinations work beyond Washington, D.C.[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105)

The New York Regional Office (NYRO) is the SEC’s largest field office. Public reporting has repeatedly described the office as having roughly 400 staff, including enforcement [attorneys](/wiki/Lawyer "Lawyer"), [accountants](/wiki/Accountant "Accountant"), investigators, and compliance examiners.[[106]](#cite_note-Reuters-Calamari-2017-106) NYRO is based in the country's main securities market hub, as New York is the world's top financial center according to the [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index").[[107]](#cite_note-Reuters-GFCI-2024-107)[[108]](#cite_note-NYOSC-Securities-2025-108) The NY field office handles a particularly large share of the SEC’s regional examinations[[109]](#cite_note-Reuters-Custody-2023-109)[[110]](#cite_note-Reuters-Robinhood-2025-110) and investigations involving regulated firms.[[111]](#cite_note-Reuters-SEC-NYC-2010-111)[[112]](#cite_note-Reuters-SEC-Reorg-2025-112)[[113]](#cite_note-Reuters-Shvartsman-2023-113)[[114]](#cite_note-Reuters-Left-2025-114)[[115]](#cite_note-Reuters-Briargate-2015-115)[[116]](#cite_note-Reuters-Enforcement-Priorities-2025-116)[[117]](#cite_note-Reuters-BFBorgers-2024-117) Many New York securities matters also proceed in parallel with criminal cases in Manhattan and Brooklyn.[[113]](#cite_note-Reuters-Shvartsman-2023-113)[[118]](#cite_note-Reuters-Hackers-2015-118)[[119]](#cite_note-Reuters-TwoSigma-2025-119) High-profile crypto cases litigated in the Southern District of New York have included *SEC v. Ripple Labs* and *SEC v. Terraform Labs*.[[120]](#cite_note-Reuters-Ripple-2022-120)[[121]](#cite_note-Reuters-Terraform-2024-121)[[120]](#cite_note-Reuters-Ripple-2022-120)[[121]](#cite_note-Reuters-Terraform-2024-121)
