### Specialized offices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Specialized offices")]

In addition to its operating divisions, the SEC includes specialized offices. These include:

* The *Office of General Counsel*, which represents the agency in federal appellate courts and provides legal advice to the commission and other SEC divisions and offices;
* The *Office of the Chief Accountant*, which serves as the commission's principal adviser on accounting and auditing matters and helps establish and interpret the SEC's accounting and auditing policy;
* The *Office of International Affairs*, which represents the SEC abroad, negotiates international enforcement information-sharing agreements, develops the SEC's international regulatory policies in areas such as mutual recognition, and helps develop international regulatory standards through organizations such as the [International Organization of Securities Commissions](/wiki/International_Organization_of_Securities_Commissions "International Organization of Securities Commissions") and the [Financial Stability Board](/wiki/Financial_Stability_Board "Financial Stability Board");
* The *Office of Information Technology*, which supports the commission and staff in information technology, including application development, infrastructure operations and engineering, user support, IT program management, capital planning, security, and enterprise architecture;
* The *[Office of Inspector General](/wiki/Office_of_Inspector_General_(United_States) "Office of Inspector General (United States)")*; and
* The *[SEC Office of the Whistleblower](/wiki/SEC_Office_of_the_Whistleblower "SEC Office of the Whistleblower")*, which administers the SEC's whistleblower program and provides information to individuals who report possible securities law violations.

## Budget

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Budget")]

Unlike the budgets of many [national security agencies](/wiki/United_States_Intelligence_Community "United States Intelligence Community"), the SEC's budget is public and goes through [a congressional](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") approval each year.[[122]](#cite_note-CRS-Intel-Budget-122)[[123]](#cite_note-CRS-SEC-Approps-123) Unlike most federal agencies, which receive direct appropriations, the SEC is not funded the way most federal agencies are. Most agencies receive their money straight from the [U.S. Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") — money that ultimately comes from federal taxes paid by the public; the SEC does not follow this funding framework. Instead, Congress sets an annual spending limit for the SEC’s operations. The agency is funded by securities transaction fees, which the SEC automatically collects indirectly under [Section 31](/wiki/Securities_Exchange_Act_of_1934#Section_31 "Securities Exchange Act of 1934") of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934.[[123]](#cite_note-CRS-SEC-Approps-123)[[124]](#cite_note-GAO-SEC-FY25-Audit-Budget-124) Broker-dealers and exchanges collect and remit Section 31 fees, which are deposited in the U.S. Treasury and offset the SEC’s congressionally authorized spending. Both the 1934 Act and the SEC describe this funding structure as "deficit neutral"[[125]](#cite_note-Reuters-SEC-deficit-neutral-125) because the agency does not rely on any taxpayer dollars.[[123]](#cite_note-CRS-SEC-Approps-123) Because Section 31 fees fluctuate with [trading volume](/wiki/Volume_(finance) "Volume (finance)"), collections can exceed the SEC's annual *budget authority* – the legal limit set by Congress on the agency's spending for a given [fiscal year](/wiki/Fiscal_year "Fiscal year").[[126]](#cite_note-CRS-FSGG-R48188-126)

The SEC also gets money from filing fees tied to stock offerings, takeover bids, and mergers, as well as from enforcement cases, including [*disgorgement*](/wiki/Disgorgement "Disgorgement") (money wrongdoers are ordered to give back), civil penalties, and related interest.[[124]](#cite_note-GAO-SEC-FY25-Audit-Budget-124) These amounts do not fund the SEC's day-to-day operations. Depending on the statute or order involved, they may be returned to harmed investors, deposited in the Investor Protection Fund, or sent to the U.S. Treasury.[[124]](#cite_note-GAO-SEC-FY25-Audit-Budget-124)

In fiscal year 2025, the SEC reported $3.328 billion in Section 31 securities transaction fees and $1.027 billion in filing fees, against total program costs of $2.4 billion.[[124]](#cite_note-GAO-SEC-FY25-Audit-Budget-124) Of the filing-fee revenue collected in fiscal year 2025, $50 million was deposited in the SEC Reserve Fund and $977 million was deposited in the [U.S. Treasury General Fund](/wiki/Treasury_General_Account "Treasury General Account").[[124]](#cite_note-GAO-SEC-FY25-Audit-Budget-124) [Public Law 119-21](/wiki/Public_Law_119-21 "Public Law 119-21") closed the SEC Reserve Fund, effective October 1, 2025.[[127]](#cite_note-CRS-IN12579-ReserveFund-127) The SEC's financial statements are audited each year by the [Government Accountability Office](/wiki/Government_Accountability_Office "Government Accountability Office").[[124]](#cite_note-GAO-SEC-FY25-Audit-Budget-124)

## Functions and operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Functions and operations")]

### Communications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Communications")]

#### Comment letters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Comment letters")]

The term *comment letter* at the SEC is used in two different ways, and its meaning depends on the context. In [rulemaking](/wiki/Rulemaking "Rulemaking"), a comment letter is a public submission from [investors](/wiki/Investor "Investor"), [academics](/wiki/Research "Research"), [companies](/wiki/Company "Company"), or other [stakeholders](/wiki/Stakeholder_(corporate) "Stakeholder (corporate)") responding to a proposed SEC rule; researchers have found that these letters can help shape how rules are revised before adoption.[[128]](#cite_note-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129) In the SEC's Division of Corporation Finance’s review of company filings, a comment letter is a communication that SEC staff send to a public company to ask questions or request changes to its disclosures[[130]](#cite_note-Ryans-CommentLetters2-130) , a process described in academic work on the SEC’s oversight of corporate reporting.[[131]](#cite_note-131)[[132]](#cite_note-132) In June 2004, the SEC announced that comment letters and company responses for selected disclosure filings made after August 1, 2004 would be posted publicly through EDGAR, rather than being available only through FOIA requests.[[130]](#cite_note-Ryans-CommentLetters2-130)[[133]](#cite_note-133) An early example of the process involved [CA, Inc.](/wiki/CA_Technologies "CA Technologies"), which received SEC staff letters in late 2001 raising accounting and revenue-recognition questions.[[134]](#cite_note-134) The [chief executive officer](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer") of CA, to whom the letter was addressed, pleaded guilty to fraud at CA in 2004.[[135]](#cite_note-nytimesgavin22-135)

#### No-action letters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: No-action letters")]

[No-action letters](/wiki/No-action_letter "No-action letter") are SEC staff responses indicating that the staff will not recommend enforcement action if the requester proceeds as described. They reflect the views of the SEC staff rather than the Commission itself and are not legally binding on either the Commission or the courts.[[136]](#cite_note-136) One example of the SEC’s use of the no-action letter between 1975 and 2007 involved the designation of [nationally recognized statistical rating organizations (NRSROs)](/wiki/Nationally_recognized_statistical_rating_organization "Nationally recognized statistical rating organization"), under which [credit rating agencies](/wiki/Credit_rating_agency "Credit rating agency") issued [ratings](/wiki/Credit_rating "Credit rating") that other financial firms could use for certain regulatory purposes.[[137]](#cite_note-137)[[138]](#cite_note-138)

### Whistleblower program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Whistleblower program")]

The SEC runs a whistleblower rewards program, which rewards individuals who report violations of [securities laws](/wiki/Securities_law "Securities law") to the SEC.[[139]](#cite_note-139) The program began in 2011 with the passage of the [Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act](/wiki/Dodd%E2%80%93Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act "Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act") and allows whistleblowers to receive 10–30% of the monetary sanctions collected in actions where the sanctions exceed $1 million.[[140]](#cite_note-140) As of the end of fiscal year 2023, the SEC had awarded almost $2 billion to nearly 400 whistleblowers through the program.[[141]](#cite_note-141) In fiscal year 2025 alone, the Commission awarded more than $60 million to 48 individual whistleblowers.[[142]](#cite_note-142) As part of the program, the SEC issues a report to Congress each year and the 2021 report is available online.[[143]](#cite_note-143)

### Coordination with criminal, financial, and foreign authorities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Coordination with criminal, financial, and foreign authorities")]

The SEC often works in parallel with criminal authorities such as the [DOJ](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") and the [FBI](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation"). GAO reported in 2007 that securities regulators, federal law-enforcement agencies, self-regulatory organizations, and the [Public Company Accounting Oversight Board](/wiki/Public_Company_Accounting_Oversight_Board "Public Company Accounting Oversight Board") shared information on securities and commodities fraud, and GAO testimony in 2002 said the SEC, DOJ, and [IRS](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service") had also coordinated on tax-scheme and internet-fraud matters.[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145)[[146]](#cite_note-146) The SEC reported about 500 investigations a year from fiscal years 2013 through 2017 in which it gave other criminal authorities access to information from its own investigations; that fell to 442 in fiscal year 2018.[[147]](#cite_note-SEC-FY20-CBJ2-147) The SEC also reports annual counts of "follow-on" administrative proceedings, in which it seeks industry bars or suspensions after another agency has already obtained a criminal conviction, civil injunction, or other order. Those counts were 143 in fiscal year 2021, 169 in fiscal year 2022, 162 in fiscal year 2023, and 93 in fiscal year 2024.[[148]](#cite_note-148)[[149]](#cite_note-149)[[150]](#cite_note-150)[[151]](#cite_note-151)

The SEC also coordinates with other U.S. agencies overseeing various financial sectors. GAO has reported that these agencies coordinate through both formal and informal channels, and that the [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve "Federal Reserve") relies on functional regulators such as the SEC when supervising broker-dealer subsidiaries and other nonbank activities within holding companies.[[152]](#cite_note-152) In 1988, [Executive Order 12631](/wiki/Working_Group_on_Financial_Markets "Working Group on Financial Markets") created the president's [Working Group on Financial Markets](/wiki/Working_Group_on_Financial_Markets "Working Group on Financial Markets"), chaired by the [secretary of the treasury](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Secretary of the Treasury") and including the chairmen of the SEC, the [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_System "Federal Reserve System"), and the [Commodity Futures Trading Commission](/wiki/Commodity_Futures_Trading_Commission "Commodity Futures Trading Commission"). Its purpose is to promote fair, orderly, efficient, and competitive financial markets while maintaining investor confidence.[[153]](#cite_note-treasury-153) The SEC as one of DOJ’s partners in a broader cross-agency effort, a public-private information sharing partnership called *Illicit Virtual Asset Notification (IVAN)*, to combat the illicit use of digital assets, their use in [illicit finance flows](/wiki/Illicit_financial_flows "Illicit financial flows"), and develop legal and regulatory recommendations for that market.[[154]](#cite_note-154)[[155]](#cite_note-155)

Within the securities sector, the SEC exercises its mandated oversight authority over self-regulatory organizations, such as [FINRA](/wiki/Financial_Industry_Regulatory_Authority "Financial Industry Regulatory Authority") and the MSRB. GAO reported in 2024 that FINRA regulates more than 3,300 securities firms dealing with the public, and that the SEC oversees FINRA through inspections and ongoing monitoring.[[156]](#cite_note-156) Another SRO under SEC oversight is the [Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board](/wiki/Municipal_Securities_Rulemaking_Board "Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board") (MSRB), which Congress created in 1975 to write rules for the municipal securities market.

The SEC also works with state securities regulators and foreign counterparts. States can enforce their own blue sky laws,[[26]](#cite_note-bluesky-26) but the [National Securities Markets Improvement Act of 1996](/wiki/National_Securities_Markets_Improvement_Act_of_1996 "National Securities Markets Improvement Act of 1996") limited state registration rules for nationally traded securities. The SEC also works with foreign regulators through [IOSCO](/wiki/International_Organization_of_Securities_Commissions "International Organization of Securities Commissions"), its multilateral information-sharing framework, and direct agreements with other countries' [securities commissions](/wiki/Securities_commission "Securities commission") to address cross-border securities misconduct.

The SEC also cross-shares information with parts of the [United States Intelligence Community](/wiki/United_States_Intelligence_Community "United States Intelligence Community"). Public reporting on that process such is limited because many of these briefings are [non-public](/wiki/Market_moving_information "Market moving information") or [classified](/wiki/Classified_information_in_the_United_States "Classified information in the United States") but a few have been reported. A later [declassified](/wiki/Declassification "Declassification") [Langley](/wiki/Langley_(CIA) "Langley (CIA)") record shows that, in 1977, the [CIA](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency")'s Rome [station chief](/wiki/Station_chief "Station chief") met with SEC Chairman [Roderick M. Hills](/wiki/Roderick_M._Hills "Roderick M. Hills") to warn that disclosing the names of [Lockheed](/wiki/Lockheed_bribery_scandals "Lockheed bribery scandals") [bribery](/wiki/Bribery "Bribery") recipients could damage U.S.–Italian relations.[[157]](#cite_note-157) *[Barron's](/wiki/Barron%27s "Barron's")* reported that beginning in 2007, SEC Chairman [Christopher Cox](/wiki/Christopher_Cox "Christopher Cox") and the other commissioners had started receiving monthly CIA briefings on [terrorists](/wiki/Terrorism "Terrorism") and other criminals active in [global capital markets](/wiki/Capital_market "Capital market").[[158]](#cite_note-158)[[159]](#cite_note-159) [Reuters](/wiki/Reuters "Reuters") and [NYT](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times") also reported that the SEC had sought [TS/SCI security clearances](/wiki/Sensitive_compartmented_information "Sensitive compartmented information") for commissioners so they could receive highly [classified information](/wiki/Classified_information_in_the_United_States "Classified information in the United States").[[160]](#cite_note-160)[[161]](#cite_note-161) At the broader federal level, including parts of the Intelligence Community, the SEC also has been part of the [National Cyber Investigative Joint Task Force](/wiki/National_Cyber_Investigative_Joint_Task_Force "National Cyber Investigative Joint Task Force") (NCIJTF), a multi-agency center for coordinating and sharing information on [cyber threat investigations](/wiki/Cyber_threat_intelligence "Cyber threat intelligence")..[[162]](#cite_note-CRS-Cyber-Incident-2017-162)[[163]](#cite_note-DOJ-OIG-Ransomware-2024-163)

## Major enforcement episodes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Major enforcement episodes")]

Main article: [Enforcement actions of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission](/wiki/Enforcement_actions_of_the_U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "Enforcement actions of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission")

This section highlights selected episodes in SEC enforcement history in which major cases, market events, or court rulings marked a clear change in how the agency pursued enforcement.

### Defining what counts as a security (1934–1953)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Defining what counts as a security (1934–1953)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Entrance_to_SEC_LCCN2016871349.jpg/330px-Entrance_to_SEC_LCCN2016871349.jpg)](/wiki/File:Entrance_to_SEC_LCCN2016871349.jpg)

Entrance to the headquarters of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission in Washington, D.C., [ca. 1937]

After the SEC’s early days, some of its defining moments were about legal coverage. The Supreme Court’s decisions in *[SEC v. W. J. Howey Co.](/wiki/SEC_v._W._J._Howey_Co. "SEC v. W. J. Howey Co.")* (1946) and *[SEC v. Ralston Purina Co.](/wiki/SEC_v._Ralston_Purina_Co. "SEC v. Ralston Purina Co.")* (1953) created jurisdictional guidelines. The cases set precedents for what securities law would turn on economic reality rather than labels, and the private-offering exemption would be confined to offerees able to fend for themselves or obtain equivalent information.[[12]](#cite_note-Seligman-TWS2-12)[[164]](#cite_note-Albert-Howey-164)[[165]](#cite_note-Fletcher-Sophisticated-165)[[166]](#cite_note-Cox-Offerings-166) In *Howey*, the split between the SEC and the defendants was about what constituted a security or an investment contract. The promoters sold small interests in a Florida [citrus-grove](/wiki/Citrus_production "Citrus production") development and argued that they were merely selling real estate, sometimes alongside a separate service contract. The SEC argued that the land sale and service arrangement were, in substance, one investment scheme aimed at buyers who lacked the desire or practical ability to cultivate and market the fruit themselves and were instead attracted by the prospect of passive returns. [The Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") agreed, holding that the arrangement was an “investment contract” because it involved an investment of money in a common enterprise with profits to come from the efforts of others.[[164]](#cite_note-Albert-Howey-164)[[12]](#cite_note-Seligman-TWS2-12) Later courts summarized *Howey* in four elements—an investment of money, in a common enterprise, with an expectation of profits, to be derived from the efforts of others—making it a durable framework for later SEC enforcement involving novel instruments and fundraising schemes, while *Ralston Purina* limited the private-offering exemption to investors able to fend for themselves or obtain equivalent information.[[164]](#cite_note-Albert-Howey-164)[[165]](#cite_note-Fletcher-Sophisticated-165)[[166]](#cite_note-Cox-Offerings-166)[[12]](#cite_note-Seligman-TWS2-12)

### Insider trading and misuse of secret information (1968–1991)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Insider trading and misuse of secret information (1968–1991)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Texas_Gulf_Sulphur_Company_%2810428906725%29.jpg/330px-Texas_Gulf_Sulphur_Company_%2810428906725%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Texas_Gulf_Sulphur_Company_(10428906725).jpg)

Texas Gulf Sulphur Company worker in Newgulf, Texas, c. 1939. The company later figured in a landmark insider-trading case.

After *Howey*, a different enforcement issue came to the front: when does an informational advantage become unlawful? That issue was at the core of the case *[SEC v. Texas Gulf Sulphur](/wiki/SEC_v._Texas_Gulf_Sulphur_Co. "SEC v. Texas Gulf Sulphur Co.")* (1968), which arose after company insiders and others traded before public disclosure of a major ore discovery in Canada. The SEC argued that anyone in possession of important nonpublic information—information a reasonable investor would consider significant and that could affect a company’s stock price— through a corporate position had to disclose it or abstain from trading, while the defendants argued for a narrower rule. [The Second Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Second_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit") made *Texas Gulf Sulphur* an early landmark for the principle that insiders in possession of market-moving nonpublic information must either disclose it or refrain from trading.[[167]](#cite_note-Langevoort-TGS-167)[[168]](#cite_note-Fairfax-TGS-168)[[12]](#cite_note-Seligman-TWS2-12)

Later Supreme Court decisions narrowed that idea further. In *[Chiarella v. United States](/wiki/Chiarella_v._United_States "Chiarella v. United States")* (1980), [the Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") held that trading on nonpublic information is not necessarily unlawful under federal securities law merely because one trader has an informational advantage; liability instead depends on a duty to disclose arising from a relationship of trust and confidence.[[167]](#cite_note-Langevoort-TGS-167)[[169]](#cite_note-Nagy-Chiarella-169) In *[Dirks v. SEC](/wiki/Dirks_v._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "Dirks v. Securities and Exchange Commission")* (1983), [the Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") held that someone who receives inside information can be liable if the insider improperly shared confidential information for some personal gain, and if the recipient knew—or should have known—that the insider was not supposed to share it.[[170]](#cite_note-Pritchard-Dirks-170) These court cases ultimately moved insider-trading law away from the broad idea that trading on secret information is wrongful, and toward a narrower rule based on whether the information was shared in violation of trust and whether the trader knew that it was done improperly. [[167]](#cite_note-Langevoort-TGS-167)[[168]](#cite_note-Fairfax-TGS-168)[[169]](#cite_note-Nagy-Chiarella-169)[[170]](#cite_note-Pritchard-Dirks-170)

### When market watchdogs failed (2001–2008)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: When market watchdogs failed (2001–2008)")]

By the early 2000s, some of the SEC’s most prominent cases focused on the market’s own watchdogs, which had failed to detect and disclose fraud in time. [Enron](/wiki/Enron "Enron") became a major example: investors were told they were seeing the results of a successful company, while regulators argued that the company’s disclosures, off-balance-sheet structures, and accounting numbers created a misleading picture, raising broader questions about whether auditors and counsel had acted as effective gatekeepers.[[171]](#cite_note-Coffee-Enron2-171) The [Global Analyst Research Settlement](/wiki/Global_Analyst_Research_Settlements "Global Analyst Research Settlements") reflected a related conflict: major firms presented securities research as objective analysis. The SEC argued that investment-banking incentives had distorted supposedly independent recommendations during the dot-com era.[[172]](#cite_note-Labhart-Analysts2-172) The [Madoff scandal](/wiki/Madoff_investment_scandal "Madoff investment scandal"), uncovered in 2008, raised the issue of whether the SEC itself had missed repeated red flags about a major Ponzi scheme.[[173]](#cite_note-Rhee-Madoff2-173)[[171]](#cite_note-Coffee-Enron2-171)[[172]](#cite_note-Labhart-Analysts2-172)

### Market measures during and after the 2008 financial crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Market measures during and after the 2008 financial crisis")]

During the 2008 financial crisis, the SEC responded through a mix of emergency market measures, crisis-related investigations, and large investor settlements. On September 19, 2008, the Commission temporarily banned short selling in 799 financial stocks, describing the order as a measure intended to restore "equilibrium" and curb "panic-driven" price declines.[[174]](#cite_note-Reuters-ShortBan-2008-174)[[175]](#cite_note-Fisch-SEC75-175) At the same time, it investigated suspected market manipulation through false rumors about financial institutions, examined trading irregularities and abusive [short-selling](/wiki/Short-selling "Short-selling") practices, and required [hedge fund](/wiki/Hedge_fund "Hedge fund") managers, broker-dealers, and institutional investors to provide sworn information about positions in [credit default swaps](/wiki/Credit_default_swap "Credit default swap").[[176]](#cite_note-Reuters-RumorCrackdown-176)[[177]](#cite_note-Bloomberg-CDS-Oath-177) The SEC also pursued large crisis-era settlements on behalf of investors in [auction rate securities](/wiki/Auction_rate_security "Auction rate security"), obtaining roughly $51 billion in relief from six financial institutions.[[175]](#cite_note-Fisch-SEC75-175)[[178]](#cite_note-Reuters-AuctionRate-2009-178)

From 2009 to 2012, the SEC brought a large number of high-profile cases tied to the [financial crisis of 2007–2008](/wiki/Financial_crisis_of_2007%E2%80%932008 "Financial crisis of 2007–2008") and its aftermath.[[179]](#cite_note-Zaring-FinancialCrisis-179)[[180]](#cite_note-Reuters-SEC-2011-Record-180) Many of these actions focused on mortgage lending, structured products, and crisis-era disclosures, including cases involving [Countrywide Financial](/wiki/Countrywide_Financial "Countrywide Financial"), [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America"), [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs"), [Citigroup](/wiki/Citigroup "Citigroup"), [State Street Bank](/wiki/State_Street_Bank "State Street Bank"), [Wachovia](/wiki/Wachovia "Wachovia"), [J.P. Morgan](/wiki/J.P._Morgan "J.P. Morgan"), [Mizuho Financial Group](/wiki/Mizuho_Financial_Group "Mizuho Financial Group"), Option One Mortgage, and former executives at [Fannie Mae](/wiki/Fannie_Mae "Fannie Mae") and [Freddie Mac](/wiki/Freddie_Mac "Freddie Mac").[[181]](#cite_note-181)[[182]](#cite_note-Reuters-IndyMac-2012-182)[[183]](#cite_note-Reuters-Mizuho-2012-183)

During the period, SEC's enforcement expanded crisis-related actions involving auction-rate securities, municipal bond reinvestment transactions, asset-backed products, and allegedly misleading public statements about loan quality, risk exposure, and the pricing or composition of complex financial instruments.[[184]](#cite_note-184)[[179]](#cite_note-Zaring-FinancialCrisis-179)[[180]](#cite_note-Reuters-SEC-2011-Record-180)

The agency also targeted wrongdoing related to insider trading, investment adviser misconduct, [Ponzi schemes](/wiki/Ponzi_scheme "Ponzi scheme"), [Foreign Corrupt Practices Act](/wiki/Foreign_Corrupt_Practices_Act "Foreign Corrupt Practices Act") cases, and market-structure violations. Major insider-trading matters included the [Galleon Group](/wiki/Galleon_Group "Galleon Group") investigation, actions against [Raj Rajaratnam](/wiki/Raj_Rajaratnam "Raj Rajaratnam") and [Rajat Gupta](/wiki/Rajat_Gupta "Rajat Gupta"), the expert-network cases, and later cases involving hedge funds, consultants, physicians, and corporate insiders.[[185]](#cite_note-Reuters-Rajaratnam-Factbox-185)[[186]](#cite_note-Reuters-ExpertNetworks-186)[[187]](#cite_note-Reuters-Advisers-2011-187) Other actions targeted cross-border accounting fraud, bribery and pay-to-play schemes, abusive short-selling and spoofing practices, misconduct by exchanges and alternative trading systems, and large retail and affinity-fraud schemes such as [Operation Broken Trust](/wiki/Operation_Broken_Trust "Operation Broken Trust") and several major Ponzi cases.[[180]](#cite_note-Reuters-SEC-2011-Record-180)[[188]](#cite_note-188)

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of major SEC enforcement actions (2009–12)](/wiki/List_of_major_SEC_enforcement_actions_(2009%E2%80%9312) "List of major SEC enforcement actions (2009–12)").

### Enforcement actions related to crypto assets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Enforcement actions related to crypto assets")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Head_of_the_Grenadian_Delegation_to_the_12th_World_Trade_Organization_Ministerial_Conference_Justin_Sun.jpg/250px-Head_of_the_Grenadian_Delegation_to_the_12th_World_Trade_Organization_Ministerial_Conference_Justin_Sun.jpg)](/wiki/File:Head_of_the_Grenadian_Delegation_to_the_12th_World_Trade_Organization_Ministerial_Conference_Justin_Sun.jpg)

[Justin Sun](/wiki/Justin_Sun "Justin Sun") in 2022. Sun later became the subject of an SEC enforcement action

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Sam_Bankman-Fried_with_%27G-Lock%27_at_MDC_Brooklyn_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Sam_Bankman-Fried_with_%27G-Lock%27_at_MDC_Brooklyn_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sam_Bankman-Fried_with_%27G-Lock%27_at_MDC_Brooklyn_(cropped).jpg)

[Sam Bankman-Fried](/wiki/Sam_Bankman-Fried "Sam Bankman-Fried") during FTX-related proceedings, December 2023.

Beginning in the late 2010s, the SEC’s approach to digital assets took two principal forms[[189]](#cite_note-189): cases testing whether token distributions fell within the securities laws[[190]](#cite_note-CornerstoneCrypto20242-190), and enforcement actions focused on fraud, disclosure failures, and market breakdowns within crypto businesses and ecosystems.[[191]](#cite_note-CornerstoneCrypto2022Fraud2-191)

The registration theory appeared early in the [Telegram](/wiki/Telegram_(software) "Telegram (software)") matter (2019–2020), where the SEC alleged an unregistered offering tied to the planned distribution of digital tokens. Telegram agreed to return investor funds and pay an $18.5 million civil penalty, making the case an early application of securities-registration principles to token distribution plans.[[192]](#cite_note-192) The same issue later produced a longer-running and higher-profile dispute in the [Ripple](/wiki/Ripple_Labs "Ripple Labs") litigation. In December 2020, the SEC sued [Ripple Labs](/wiki/Ripple_Labs "Ripple Labs") and executives [Bradley Garlinghouse](/wiki/Brad_Garlinghouse "Brad Garlinghouse") and [Christian A. Larsen](/wiki/Chris_Larsen "Chris Larsen"), alleging that [XRP](/wiki/XRP_Ledger "XRP Ledger") sales constituted an unregistered securities offering.[[193]](#cite_note-193) In July 2023, the district court held that Ripple’s institutional XRP sales were unregistered investment contracts, while programmatic sales on trading platforms and certain other distributions were not.[[194]](#cite_note-194) In August 2024, the court entered final judgment imposing a $125,035,150 civil penalty and an injunction, and the parties later dismissed their [Second Circuit appeals](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Second_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit"), leaving that judgment in place.[[195]](#cite_note-195)[[196]](#cite_note-196) The case later became part of a broader debate over the SEC’s retreat from crypto litigation after President Trump returned to office[[197]](#cite_note-197)[[198]](#cite_note-198), a shift described by [the *New York Times*](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times") as unusually rapid, though SEC and administration officials denied that political favoritism drove the change.[[199]](#cite_note-NYT-2025-12-142-199)[[200]](#cite_note-Reuters-2026-02-12-Atkins-Criticism2-200)

A second pattern involved fraud, disclosure, and control failures inside crypto businesses and ecosystems. After the [FTX](/wiki/FTX "FTX") collapse in 2022, the SEC charged [Sam Bankman-Fried](/wiki/Sam_Bankman-Fried "Sam Bankman-Fried") with defrauding equity investors in the platform, making the case a major reference point in debates over custody[[201]](#cite_note-201)[[202]](#cite_note-202)[[203]](#cite_note-203), internal controls, and affiliated trading risks in crypto markets.[[204]](#cite_note-204) The SEC’s civil case was stayed pending the parallel criminal proceeding, in which Bankman-Fried was later sentenced to 25 years’ imprisonment.[[205]](#cite_note-205)[[206]](#cite_note-206) The SEC’s [Terraform Labs](/wiki/Terra_(blockchain) "Terra (blockchain)") matter, filed in 2023, reflected similar concerns in a different form. There the agency alleged misleading statements and fraud tied to [Terraform’s](/wiki/Terra_(blockchain) "Terra (blockchain)") marketed stability mechanisms and ecosystem representations[[207]](#cite_note-207)[[208]](#cite_note-208)[[209]](#cite_note-209), illustrating how disclosure theories and market-integrity concerns could converge in digital-asset products.[[210]](#cite_note-210)

## Rulemaking and oversight issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Rulemaking and oversight issues")]

In the 2020s, the SEC’s rulemaking agenda focused heavily on disclosure and market structure, including cybersecurity, climate-related reporting, and the treatment of new trading and settlement technologies. These initiatives drew sustained debate over the scope of the Commission’s disclosure authority, the compliance burden on issuers and intermediaries, and how existing securities rules should apply to changing market infrastructure.[[211]](#cite_note-211)[[212]](#cite_note-212)

### Disclosure rulemaking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Disclosure rulemaking")]

The SEC adopted major disclosure rules on cybersecurity and climate-related reporting in the 2020s. The cybersecurity rules required public companies to disclose material cyber incidents and certain information about cybersecurity risk management and governance, while the climate-disclosure rule required certain climate-related disclosures but was narrower than originally proposed and was later stayed amid litigation. Together, these rulemakings reflected a broader effort to expand standardized disclosure into newer categories of risk, while also highlighting disagreement over the limits of the SEC’s authority and the practical burden of compliance.[[213]](#cite_note-213)[[214]](#cite_note-214)[[215]](#cite_note-215)

### Market structure and digital infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Market structure and digital infrastructure")]

The SEC also continued broader work on equity-market structure and on how securities regulation applies to new digital infrastructure. These efforts included rule changes tied to order execution and retail trading, as well as staff action involving tokenized securities and post-trade infrastructure. More broadly, they formed part of an ongoing debate over how existing securities rules should apply to technological change in trading, custody, clearing, and settlement.[[216]](#cite_note-216)[[217]](#cite_note-217)

### Regulatory criticisms and oversight failures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Regulatory criticisms and oversight failures")]

#### Enforcement practices and accountability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Enforcement practices and accountability")]

The SEC has periodically faced criticism over the pace and aggressiveness of its enforcement decisions, especially in matters involving prominent Wall Street firms or executives. A 2007 U.S. Senate report on the dismissal of SEC enforcement lawyer [Gary J. Aguirre](/wiki/Gary_J._Aguirre "Gary J. Aguirre") during the [Pequot Capital Management](/wiki/Pequot_Capital_Management "Pequot Capital Management") insider-trading investigation called for reforms in the agency’s enforcement culture, and later reporting after the [financial crisis of 2007–2008](/wiki/Financial_crisis_of_2007%E2%80%932008 "Financial crisis of 2007–2008") described broader criticism that the Commission had been too cautious in pursuing senior executives and large financial institutions.[[218]](#cite_note-SenateAguirreReport-218)[[219]](#cite_note-BloombergTimidSEC-219)

#### Madoff-related oversight failures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Madoff-related oversight failures")]

The SEC’s handling of the [Bernard Madoff](/wiki/Bernard_Madoff "Bernard Madoff") fraud became one of the agency’s most widely cited oversight failures. In 2009, the SEC’s Office of Inspector General found that the agency had received credible warnings, conducted multiple examinations and investigations, and still failed to uncover Madoff’s Ponzi scheme, prompting criticism of the Commission’s investigative judgment, follow-up, and internal controls.[[220]](#cite_note-SEC-OIG-Madoff-220) Former chairman [Christopher Cox](/wiki/Christopher_Cox "Christopher Cox") publicly acknowledged multiple failures in the agency’s response, and the episode became a lasting reference point in later debates over SEC oversight and enforcement effectiveness.[[221]](#cite_note-FTCoxMadoff-221)

## Media portrayal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Media portrayal")]

Main article: [U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission portrayal in media](/wiki/U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission_portrayal_in_media "U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission portrayal in media")

The SEC has appeared in many [films](/wiki/Film "Film") and [television series](/wiki/Television_series "Television series") about [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street"), corporate misconduct, and [financial fraud](/wiki/Financial_fraud "Financial fraud"). In these portrayals, the agency typically appears as a law enforcement body, investigating [insider trading](/wiki/Insider_trading "Insider trading"), [money laundering](/wiki/Money_laundering "Money laundering"), accounting fraud, or some other money-related wrongdoing.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Leonardo_DiCaprio_avp_2013.jpg/250px-Leonardo_DiCaprio_avp_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_DiCaprio_avp_2013.jpg)

[Leonardo DiCaprio](/wiki/Leonardo_DiCaprio "Leonardo DiCaprio"), who portrays [Jordan Belfort](/wiki/Jordan_Belfort "Jordan Belfort") in *[The Wolf of Wall Street](/wiki/The_Wolf_of_Wall_Street_(2013_film) "The Wolf of Wall Street (2013 film)")*.

A notable example is the *[Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street_(1987_film) "Wall Street (1987 film)")* (1987), directed by [Oliver Stone](/wiki/Oliver_Stone "Oliver Stone"), in which the SEC serves as a check against what the movie portrays as culture of aggressive and morally dubious deal-making. In the movie, Bud Fox ([Charlie Sheen](/wiki/Charlie_Sheen "Charlie Sheen")), an ambitious young broker, advances by passing [material non-public information](/wiki/Material_non-public_information "Material non-public information") to corporate raider Gordon Gekko ([Michael Douglas](/wiki/Michael_Douglas "Michael Douglas")), then turns against him as the SEC investigation closes in. In *[The Wolf of Wall Street](/wiki/The_Wolf_of_Wall_Street_(2013_film) "The Wolf of Wall Street (2013 film)")* (2013), the SEC's investigation forms part of Jordan Belfort's downfall as the film depicts [penny stock](/wiki/Penny_stock "Penny stock") manipulation and the rise and collapse of [Stratton Oakmont](/wiki/Stratton_Oakmont "Stratton Oakmont").[[222]](#cite_note-222) In [Steven Soderbergh](/wiki/Steven_Soderbergh "Steven Soderbergh")'s *[Side Effects](/wiki/Side_Effects_(2013_film) "Side Effects (2013 film)")* (2013), SEC charges for securities fraud are part of the plot's climax, including a recorded meeting in which Emily Taylor cooperates with authorities against Dr. Victoria Siebert.

On television, *[Arrested Development](/wiki/Arrested_Development "Arrested Development")* (2003–2019) uses the SEC as part of the Bluth family's corporate-fraud storyline, with SEC agents on [raid](/wiki/Police_raid "Police raid") boats appearing early in the series. [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix")'s 2024 documentary *[Bitconned](/wiki/Bitconned "Bitconned")* gives the SEC a prominent role in the collapse of Centra Tech, a cryptocurrency venture that fraudulently raised $32 million in an [initial coin offering](/wiki/Initial_coin_offering "Initial coin offering").[[223]](#cite_note-223) The documentary recounts the company's false claims about its "Centra Card", supposed commercial partnerships, and fake executive biographies, and presents the SEC investigation and subpoenas as a turning point in the scheme's collapse.[[224]](#cite_note-224)[[225]](#cite_note-225)

## Selected federal securities statutes and SEC regulations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Selected federal securities statutes and SEC regulations")]

* **Foundational securities statutes**: [Securities Act of 1933](/wiki/Securities_Act_of_1933 "Securities Act of 1933"); [Securities Exchange Act of 1934](/wiki/Securities_Exchange_Act_of_1934 "Securities Exchange Act of 1934"); [Trust Indenture Act of 1939](/wiki/Trust_Indenture_Act_of_1939 "Trust Indenture Act of 1939"); [Investment Advisers Act of 1940](/wiki/Investment_Advisers_Act_of_1940 "Investment Advisers Act of 1940"); [Investment Company Act of 1940](/wiki/Investment_Company_Act_of_1940 "Investment Company Act of 1940").
* **Takeovers, market structure, and financial modernization**: [Williams Act](/wiki/Williams_Act "Williams Act") (1968); [Gramm–Leach–Bliley Act](/wiki/Gramm%E2%80%93Leach%E2%80%93Bliley_Act "Gramm–Leach–Bliley Act") (1999); [Commodity Futures Modernization Act of 2000](/wiki/Commodity_Futures_Modernization_Act_of_2000 "Commodity Futures Modernization Act of 2000").
* **Corporate governance, credit ratings, and post-crisis reform**: [Sarbanes–Oxley Act](/wiki/Sarbanes%E2%80%93Oxley_Act "Sarbanes–Oxley Act") (2002); [Credit Rating Agency Reform Act](/wiki/Credit_Rating_Agency_Reform_Act "Credit Rating Agency Reform Act") (2006); [Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act](/wiki/Dodd%E2%80%93Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act "Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act") (2010) (including the [Volcker Rule](/wiki/Volcker_Rule "Volcker Rule")).
* **Cross-border audit oversight and digital-asset legislation**: [Holding Foreign Companies Accountable Act](/wiki/Holding_Foreign_Companies_Accountable_Act "Holding Foreign Companies Accountable Act") (2020); [GENIUS Act](/wiki/GENIUS_Act "GENIUS Act") (2025).[[226]](#cite_note-226)
* **SEC regulations**: rules adopted under the federal securities laws, codified primarily in [Title 17 of the Code of Federal Regulations](/wiki/Title_17_of_the_Code_of_Federal_Regulations "Title 17 of the Code of Federal Regulations").
